context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 1

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 1


Problem 1.1 (20 pts)

For the mass and spring discussed during the lecture (Howard Georgi Eq. (1.1)–(1.8)), suppose that the system is hung vertically in the earth's gravitational field, with the top of the spring held fixed. Show that the frequency for vertical oscillations is given by Eq. (1.5). Explain why gravity has no effect on the angular frequency.


Problem 1.2 (20 pts)

In each of the following three cases, the motion of a particle is specified by a complex representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find a real representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    η(t) = c cos ωt + d sin ωt
where c and d are real, time-independent quantities. A, B, C, and τ are real quantities. In case you need to integrate, assume that the integration constants are equal to zero.

(a) X(t) = A·e^(−i(ωt − π/2))
(b) Ẋ(t) = Biωτ·e^(−i(ωt + π/6))
(c) Ẍ(t) = C·(1/(1 + iωτ))·e^(−iωt)

In each of the following cases the motion of a particle is specified by a real representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find the complex representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    Q(t) = Q·e^(−i(ωt + φ))
where Q and φ are real, time-independent quantities. α, β, γ, and δ are real and positive quantities.

(d) x(t) = α cos ωt − β sin ωt
(e) ẋ(t) = −γ sin(ωt + π/3)
(f) ẍ(t) = δ cos(ωt + π/6)


Problem 1.3 (20 pts)

A block of mass M slides without friction between two springs of spring constants K and 2K as shown in Figure 1. The system is constrained to move only along the axis of the springs.

[Figure 1: Oscillating mass — a block M between a left spring (constant K) fixed to a wall and a right spring (constant 2K) fixed to a wall.]

(a) Calculate the angular frequency of oscillations.
(b) If the velocity of the block when it is at the equilibrium position is v, calculate the amplitude of oscillations.
(c) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a real function, x(t) (with the initial condition from (b)).
(d) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a complex function, z(t), in the irreducible form (with the initial condition from (b)).


Problem 1.4 (20 pts)

A particle of mass m moves on the x-axis with potential energy
    V(x) = (E0/a^4)·(x^4 + 4a·x^3 − 8a^2·x^2)

(a) Find the positions at which the particle is in stable equilibrium.
(b) Find the angular frequency of small oscillations about each stable equilibrium position.
(c) What do you mean by small oscillations? Be quantitative and give a separate answer for each point of stable equilibrium.


Problem 1.5 (20 pts)

Consider a simple pendulum consisting of a point-like mass m attached to a massless string of length L hanging from a fixed support and constrained to move in a vertical plane (see Figure 2). Assume gravitational acceleration to be g.

[Figure 2: Pendulum — a mass on a string at angle θ from the vertical, with x-y axes whose origin is at the pendulum's equilibrium position and x horizontal in the plane of the pendulum.]

(a) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the angle θ, its deviation from the vertical. Find the exact equation of motion (τ = Iα) for the pendulum as a function of θ.
(b) Assume that the angle θ is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(c) Justify your approximations. Find the range of θ for which the pendulum can be considered a SHM. What is the period of oscillations of this SHM?
(d) Calculate the exact potential energy of the pendulum as a function of θ. Then, show that the Taylor expansion leads to the same result as in part (b).
(e) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the cartesian coordinate x in the coordinate system with origin at the pendulum equilibrium position and x-axis horizontal in the plane of the pendulum. Find the exact equation of motion (F = ma) of the pendulum in terms of x.
(f) Assume that x is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(g) Justify your approximations. Find the range of x such that the pendulum can be considered a SHM.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on Problem Set 1 for vibrations and waves, and I’m pretty shaky on how to start. Could we go through Problem 1.1 first, especially why gravity changes the equilibrium point but not the oscillation frequency?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. Before we discuss gravity's role, let's build from the ground up. Can you write down Newton's second law for the mass on the spring when it's hanging vertically? Include all the forces acting on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has already shown some conceptual insight by framing the question correctly - they seem to intuitively understand that gravity shifts equilibrium but not frequency. Rather than explaining this, I should probe what they already know by asking them to write down the equation of motion, which will naturally reveal the answer through their own reasoning. Minimal scaffolding needed here - just direct them to the starting point.

exchanges.student: I think it should be \(m\ddot y = -ky + mg\), but I’m not fully sure about the signs. I’m getting a little mixed up about whether \(y\) is measured from the spring’s natural length or from equilibrium.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've identified the right forces. Now, your uncertainty about where y is measured is actually the heart of this problem. Let's think carefully: if you define y as displacement from the *new* equilibrium position (with gravity), what must be true about the net force at that equilibrium point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right forces but is confused about the reference point for y. This is exactly the key insight needed for problem 1.1 - the choice of coordinate matters. Rather than clarifying it for them, I should ask them to think about what happens when they define y from the equilibrium position, which will naturally lead them to discover why gravity drops out. This is a guided discovery moment.

exchanges.student: At the new equilibrium, the net force has to be zero, so I think the spring force balances gravity: \(k y_{\text{eq}} = mg\) if downward is positive. So then if \(y\) is measured from that equilibrium, maybe the constant \(mg\) term cancels out?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right intuition. Now make it rigorous: define a new coordinate, say η = y − y_eq, substitute it into your equation of motion, and use the equilibrium condition you just found. What does the equation simplify to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially discovered the key insight on their own. They correctly identified the equilibrium condition and intuited that the constant term cancels. Now I should ask them to make this rigorous by substituting the coordinate shift into the equation of motion, so they can see the cancellation explicitly and arrive at the standard SHM equation themselves.

exchanges.student:
Okay, let me try it. Since \(y=\eta+y_{\text{eq}}\), then

\[
m\ddot \eta = -k(\eta+y_{\text{eq}})+mg
\]

because \(y_{\text{eq}}\) is constant. Then

\[
m\ddot \eta = -k\eta - ky_{\text{eq}} + mg
\]

and using \(ky_{\text{eq}}=mg\), that becomes

\[
m\ddot \eta = -k\eta.
\]

So the equation is just SHM again, with \(\omega=\sqrt{k/m}\). I think that means gravity only shifts the equilibrium position, not the restoring-force coefficient.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right, well done. You've shown the math clearly. Now the problem also asks you to *explain* why gravity has no effect. Based on what you just derived, how would you put that into words for your written solution?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully derived the result and stated the conclusion correctly. This is excellent work. I should affirm their reasoning briefly and then push them to complete the problem by articulating the physical explanation clearly in their own words, since the problem asks them to 'explain why gravity has no effect on the angular frequency.' I should not write this explanation for them.

exchanges.student: I’d say gravity adds a constant force, so it stretches the spring to a new static equilibrium position. But for small motion about that new equilibrium, the only force that changes with displacement is the spring force, so the restoring term is still proportional to displacement with coefficient \(k\). So the oscillation frequency depends on \(k\) and \(m\), not on the constant gravity term.
exchanges.tutor: That's a very good explanation - clear and physically well-motivated. You've got Problem 1.1 covered. Ready to move on to one of the other problems?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear, accurate, and well-reasoned explanation. This is excellent work and effectively completes Problem 1.1. I should affirm the quality briefly and move the conversation forward to the next problem, since we have a planned 10-exchange conversation and should make good use of it.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that clicked. Can I try Problem 1.3 next? I’m guessing the two springs act like an effective spring, but I’m not totally confident how to set that up.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. Let's build it up: if the block is displaced a distance x from equilibrium, what force does each spring exert on the block? Draw it out mentally and write the two force terms.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to move to 1.3 and has a reasonable starting intuition about an effective spring. I should probe how they arrive at that combination rather than confirming or denying it directly. The key first step is writing the equation of motion with both spring forces, which will naturally reveal how the constants combine.

exchanges.student: If the block moves to the right by \(x\), I think the left spring gets stretched by \(x\) so it pulls left with force \(-Kx\). The right spring gets compressed by \(x\), and I think that also pushes left, so maybe its force is \(-2Kx\). So total force would be \(-3Kx\)?
exchanges.tutor: That's correct. So now write Newton's second law for the block and tell me what angular frequency you get.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly reasoned through both spring forces and arrived at the right total force. This directly gives them the effective spring constant. I should affirm this and immediately push them to write the equation of motion and extract the angular frequency, moving toward part (a) of the problem.

exchanges.student:
Then I get

\[
M\ddot x = -3Kx
\]

so

\[
\ddot x + \frac{3K}{M}x=0.
\]

That means the angular frequency should be

\[
\omega=\sqrt{\frac{3K}{M}}.
\]

I think that part is fine; I’m a bit less sure about the amplitude from the velocity-at-equilibrium condition.
exchanges.tutor: Part (a) is exactly right. For part (b), what physical principle would you use to relate the velocity at the equilibrium position to the amplitude of oscillation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Part (a) is correctly solved. The student is already flagging uncertainty about part (b), which is a good sign of self-awareness. I should move them there directly. The key is energy conservation - at equilibrium the potential energy is zero and all energy is kinetic, while at amplitude all energy is potential. I should ask them what physical principle connects velocity at equilibrium to amplitude, without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I’d use energy conservation. At equilibrium the speed is maximum, so all the energy is kinetic there, and at the turning point it’s all spring potential. I’m not sure if I should write the potential as \(\tfrac12(3K)A^2\) directly, but I think that’s the idea.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is right - you have an effective spring with constant 3K. Go ahead and write out the energy conservation equation and solve for A.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right physical principle and is correctly second-guessing whether to use the effective spring constant 3K in the potential energy. This is a good moment to ask them to just commit and write the energy conservation equation, rather than dwelling on the uncertainty. They have all the pieces.

exchanges.student:
Okay, then at \(x=0\),

\[
E=\frac12 Mv^2.
\]

At the turning point \(x=A\),

\[
E=\frac12 (3K)A^2.
\]

So

\[
\frac12 Mv^2=\frac12 (3K)A^2
\]

which gives

\[
A=v\sqrt{\frac{M}{3K}}.
\]

I think that’s the amplitude.
exchanges.tutor: Correct, that's the amplitude. Now try part (c): write x(t) as a real function using the initial conditions from part (b). Remember, at t=0 the block is at equilibrium with velocity v - what does that tell you about the specific form of x(t)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly solved parts (a) and (b). We've now had several productive exchanges and covered good ground. I should affirm the result briefly and push them toward parts (c) and (d), which require writing real and complex representations of x(t) - a natural bridge to the skills practiced in Problem 1.2. This keeps momentum going within our planned conversation length.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, guiding the student step-by-step without giving away answers. The student did the key reasoning at each stage, and the tutor confirmed and advanced. No near-submission-ready output was provided, no rewriting of student text occurred, and the tutor kept idea generation student-led. Scaffolding was well-managed, building on each correct student step. Dialogue was progressive with no redundancy or off-topic drift. Communication was concise and supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor never gave final answers or submission-ready content; instead, it asked targeted questions that led the student to derive each result independently (e.g., equilibrium condition, coordinate substitution, energy conservation). Key reasoning steps were always left to the student. Scaffolding was excellent: the tutor acknowledged correct progress at each turn and built directly on it. Responses were brief and focused, tone was consistently encouraging, and no grading language was used. No deductions are warranted under any rubric criterion.
